ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general eggplant food product class; no specific stir-fried brinjal entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ covers cooked aubergine/eggplant as the closest defensible match for stir-fried brinjal preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for stir-fried brinjal; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side dish serving of stir-fried brinjal in Singapore is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a small bowl or side plate portion served alongside rice.

nutrient_source.title: Stir Fried Brinjal — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Stir Fried Brinjal (F14080038). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; eggplant/brinjal analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for stir-fried brinjal in the University of Sydney GI Database. Brinjal (eggplant) is consistently classified as a low-GI vegetable with GI approximately 15 based on analogous eggplant entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 11.93) / 100 ≈ 1.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 266 mg per 100 g
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 266 mg/100 g is in the borderline-moderate range for hypertension management; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–120 g per meal and request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce during preparation to bring sodium intake closer to 200 mg per 100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~15) with moderate carbohydrate (7.95 g/100 g) and good dietary fibre (3.5 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-fried brinjal has a low GI (~15) and a glycemic load of approximately 1.8 per 150 g serving, making it suitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes; however, oil absorption during stir-frying increases caloric density, so portion control and minimal oil preparation are advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Fat content at 10.97 g/100 g with borderline sodium at 266 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The relatively high fat content (10.97 g/100 g) from oil absorption during stir-frying and borderline sodium (266 mg/100 g) warrant portion moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease; preparation with minimal oil and reduced sodium condiments is recommended to improve the cardiovascular risk profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade alkaloids (solanine) in brinjal and chili
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Brinjal is a nightshade vegetable containing solanine, which may exacerbate joint inflammation in susceptible individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; patients with confirmed nightshade sensitivity should avoid this dish and consult their rheumatologist regarding dietary nightshade restriction.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried brinjal (also known as stir-fried eggplant or aubergine) is a popular Singaporean vegetable side dish in which brinjal is cooked in hot oil with aromatics such as garlic, chili, and soy-based sauces until tender and fragrant. The dish is characterised by a soft, slightly silky texture and a savoury, mildly smoky flavour from the wok heat. Brinjal absorbs oil readily during stir-frying, which elevates the fat content to approximately 10.97 g per 100 g and the energy to 136 kcal per 100 g despite the vegetable itself being low in calories. The dish provides moderate carbohydrate (7.95 g/100 g), low protein (1.4 g/100 g), good dietary fibre (3.5 g/100 g), and a borderline sodium level of 266 mg/100 g from soy sauce and oyster sauce. The glycemic index is low (~15), making it suitable for blood glucose management. It is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-friendly. Clinically, it is appropriate for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its low GI and fibre content, but sodium should be monitored for hypertension. Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should exercise caution as brinjal is a nightshade vegetable containing solanine. Commonly served as a side dish with steamed rice in Singaporean hawker centres and home cooking.
common_user_queries: How many calories in stir-fried eggplant?
Is brinjal good for high blood pressure?
What is the GI of brinjal?
Can people with rheumatoid arthritis eat brinjal?
How much sodium is in stir-fried brinjal?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F14080038) with a confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singaporean dishes. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous stir-fried eggplant data as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for eggplant/brinjal.
notes: Saturated fat (1.5 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked eggplant with oil (closest approved-source equivalent) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this item. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid profiles, micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this specific stir-fried brinjal preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. No core macro (protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g) is zero; all are non-zero values from the HPB source. GI is an analogous estimate (~15) based on eggplant entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct stir-fried brinjal GI measurement was found in approved sources.
